extracted_information: The provided web content details 16 API security best practices, focusing heavily on authentication and authorization using OAuth2 and JWTs, gateway usage, token management, and general security principles for cloud applications. It covers topics such as centralizing OAuth, internal vs. external JWT use, token exchange, coarse-grained (scopes) and fine-grained (claims) access control, zero trust, JWT validation, key distribution with JWKS, protection of all APIs, and secure token handling by clients. Rate limiting is mentioned as a feature of API gateways. HTTPS is highlighted as part of zero-trust. Input validation and security headers are not explicitly detailed as separate best practices.

features.name: API Gateway Usage
features.description: Always put APIs behind a gateway to centralize traffic features including security (rate limiting, blocking malicious clients, proper logging), path/header rewriting, and business metrics gathering. Eases feature addition/fixing.

features.name: Central OAuth Server
features.description: A centralized OAuth authorization server should always issue access and refresh tokens. It handles complex processes like client authentication, user authentication, client authorization, and token signing, ensuring consistency and manageability.

features.name: JSON Web Tokens (JWT) Usage
features.description: Use JWTs internally to leverage claim information for informed business decisions (e.g., caller permissions, data access). For external exposure, use opaque tokens instead of JWTs to protect sensitive data and prevent breaking changes if claims change. Phantom token or split token approaches can translate opaque tokens to JWTs internally via an API gateway.

details.internal_use_case: Leverage claim information for business decisions

features.name: Token Exchange
features.description: Use the token exchange flow when services call other services. Instead of forwarding the original access token (which grants all permissions), obtain a new token with just enough information for the receiving service to authorize the request, preventing token reuse in unauthorized contexts. Especially important for requests crossing security boundaries.

details.purpose: Limit permissions when sharing tokens between services
details.method: Obtain new, specific tokens via token exchange flow

features.name: Coarse-Grained Access Control with Scopes
features.description: OAuth scopes limit the capabilities of an access token, reducing attacker power if credentials are stolen. Token scope verification should be done at the API gateway to limit the attack surface and malicious traffic. Used for high-level access control, e.g., checking if a request can query a resource or if a client can use a specific Content-Type.

features.name: Fine-Grained Access Control with Claims
features.description: Implement fine-grained access control at the API level using claims. This complements gateway-level controls, ensuring the API rejects malicious requests even if they bypass the gateway. Focuses on securing the API from a business perspective: verifying endpoint access, caller's rights to data, and data returned based on caller's identity. Addresses Broken Object-Level Authorization (BOLA).

details.function: Verify endpoint access, caller's data rights, and returned information based on identity

features.name: Zero Trust Architecture
features.description: APIs should limit trust to incoming traffic. Use HTTPS for all API traffic, including internal service-to-service communication, to prevent sniffing. Services should always verify incoming JWTs, even if transformed by a gateway, to mitigate bypass. Access should be denied by default, with claims-based access control allowing access based on concrete authorization policies.

principles: Use HTTPS for all traffic (internal and external)
Deny access by default, allow based on claims-based policies

features.name: Standardized JWT Validation
features.description: Create and reuse libraries for JWT validation across teams to ensure consistent security and quicker bug fixes. Standardizing the process prevents individual implementation errors and improves threat resolution.

details.recommendation: Create or reuse libraries for JWT validation

features.name: Avoid Mixing Authentication Methods
features.description: Do not mix authentication methods with different strength levels (e.g., basic authentication vs. multi-factor) for the same resource, as this can create security risks and API abuse vectors.

details.caution: Avoid mixing authentication methods of different strengths for the same resource

features.name: Protect All APIs
features.description: All APIs, including internal ones, should have authentication and authorization controls. Security by obscurity is ineffective; an API with a complex name or obscure Content-Type is still vulnerable.

details.caution: Security by obscurity is not recommended

features.name: Internal JWT Issuance
features.description: For internal clients operating only within the network, the OAuth server can issue JWTs directly to avoid unnecessary token translations. However, if tokens leave the network, they should be hidden behind opaque tokens.

details.condition: Clients operating only inside the network
details.restriction: JWTs must not leave the network

features.name: JSON Web Key Sets (JWKS) for Key Distribution
features.description: Obtain public keys for JWT verification from a JWKS endpoint exposed by the OAuth server. APIs should cache downloaded keys but query the JWKS endpoint for unknown signing keys. This enables simple, seamless key rotation by the OAuth server.

details.method: Obtain public keys from JWKS endpoint
details.behavior: Cache keys, query for unknown keys

features.name: Continuous Auditing and Governance
features.description: Implement ongoing security audits, peer reviews, and governance processes (e.g., dedicated API experts or guilds) throughout the API lifecycle to maintain high security and design standards.

details.goal: Maintain high security and design standards

features.name: Centralized Claims Management
features.description: Manage claims centrally via the OAuth server to control which claims appear in tokens. This is crucial for privacy and security, ensuring that APIs only use claims asserted by the centralized server and do not leak excess data.

features.name: Abuse Detection and Monitoring
features.description: Gather metrics and log API usage to detect unwanted behavior, such as requests iterating over IDs, unexpected headers/data, or customers circumventing rate limits. API abuse can be as damaging as a breach.

indicators_of_abuse: Requests with unexpected headers or data
Customers creating many clients to circumvent rate limits

features.name: Secure Token Handling by Clients
features.description: Clients must securely handle access tokens. While mobile and backend clients can store tokens securely, browser-based applications are challenging. The OAuth for Browser-Based Apps specification recommends keeping tokens out of the browser, often using a backend-for-frontend or token handler pattern with secure cookie-based sessions.

details.recommendation_for_browser_apps: Keep tokens out of the browser

references: https://owasp.org/API-Security/editions/2023/en/0xa1-broken-object-level-authorization/ (OWASP Top 10 API Security Risks: Broken Object-Level Authorization)
